[G3 child-coverage — container review of parent 1f19-adf2-ffb2-4b09 paired with this child (S1 Faithful, lock-free snapshot materialization core)]

Audited this child's contribution to the parent's acceptance/success criteria; every absence cross-checked against the WHOLE sibling roster (S2 70c7, S3 fa49, S4 30b3, S5 b7fa, S6 1ba5). This child is the materialization-core slice; the union of children covers every parent criterion. No uncovered parent criterion. Empty G3 findings list.

4-bucket coverage map (parent AC -> covering child/children; this child's role called out):
- Parent AC1 (no attested gate reads the working tree; all read a FAITHFUL snapshot materialized at the pinned SHA) — FULLY covered, with THIS child as the primary owner: its AC1 (materialize(ref) byte-matches the committed tree INCLUDING export-ignore files, faithful not git-archive) + its AC2 (LFS-pointer detection, submodule handling) deliver the faithful-snapshot half; S3 (fa49) re-roots the gates onto that snapshot. Heart of this child. Clean.
- Parent AC2 (signed verdict records verified_at_sha + reproducible over identical content) — FULLY covered by S4 (30b3, record+sign+in-toto) with the IMMUTABLE-SHA basis produced by THIS child (resolve ref -> immutable SHA against origin). STRUCTURAL w.r.t. this child: it supplies the pinned SHA but does not sign. Not an absence (S4 covers signing).
- Parent AC3 (local mode reads in-place checkout AND cannot produce a signed verdict) — covered by S3 (in-place read) + S4 (signing bar). THIS child only reflects local-mode in its fetch-error path (its AC5: attested fails closed, local still runs). STRUCTURAL w.r.t. the sign/read split here. Not an absence.
- Parent AC4 (concurrent gates on same/different SHAs run without git-lock contention or cross-op corruption; no worktrees; atomic population; delete-on-last-close) — FULLY covered, SPLIT cleanly: THIS child owns the no-worktree + atomic-population + no index.lock/config.lock-contention half (its AC3 atomic all-or-nothing population + crash leaves only tmp/; its AC6 concurrent materialize of different SHAs do not contend on index.lock/config.lock; the materialize core uses GIT_INDEX_FILE temp index + dir-rename, no worktree). S2 (70c7) owns single-flight + delete-on-last-close. No double-implementation; no gap.
- Parent AC5 (snapshot cache + janitor reclaim disk under pressure without disrupting in-flight reads) — FULLY covered by S2 (70c7). Explicitly OUT of this child's scope ("Out: the cache/janitor (S2)"). STRUCTURAL w.r.t. this child; claims none. Not an absence.
- Parent AC6 (private-repo fetch failure surfaces a descriptive, actionable credential error) — FULLY covered with THIS child as the primary owner: its AC5 (a private-repo fetch with missing credentials raises a descriptive, actionable error; attested mode fails closed) is the implementation; S6 (1ba5 AC2) documents the message + remedy + fail-closed/local-still-runs. Covered-by-impl + named-doc-consumer. Clean.
- Parent AC7 (portable: no external tar; no hardcoded /tmp; flock+mkdir fallback) — FULLY covered with THIS child as the primary owner: its AC4 (no external tar binary; no hardcoded /tmp; runs on macOS + Linux) + its portability section (tempfile.gettempdir/mkdtemp 0700, REBAR_GATE_TMPDIR override, fcntl.flock with atomic-mkdir fallback) deliver all three sub-clauses; S6 documents REBAR_GATE_TMPDIR + the EFS/NFS flock caveat (its AC5). Clean.
- Parent AC8 (all six child stories closed; make lint + full test suite green) — STRUCTURAL/meta epic-completion criterion; covered by the existence of all six children (this child is story #1 of 6 in the roster) + each child's own green-bar obligation. Not an absence at this child.

Success-criteria narrative ("every code-reading gate verifies a client-specified, immutable, reproducible snapshot of the project at a pinned SHA — never the server's mutable checkout — safely under concurrent use ...; signed verdicts reproducible and bound to verified_at_sha; unsigned local preserves verify-before-push") decomposes onto AC1-AC4/AC7. The "immutable, reproducible snapshot at a pinned SHA, faithful, never the mutable checkout" + "safely under concurrent use" clauses are precisely this child's core deliverable (its AC1/AC3/AC6); the "signed/reproducible/verified_at_sha" and "unsigned local" clauses are carried by S4/S3 atop the SHA this child pins. No uncovered obligation introduced.

Note: parts of this child's "What" explicitly delegate to named consumers — descriptive credential message detail to S6 (its AC5 "see S6"), and cache/janitor + gate-wiring + signing + surfaces out to S2/S3/S4/S5. Each delegated obligation is claimed by the named sibling (S6 AC2; S2; S3; S4; S5) — covered-by-named-consumer, not absences.

Note (scope, not a G3 finding): the existing G4 review on sibling S4 (30b3) flags a seam/dependency-gap around routing the pinned-SHA snapshot handle into plan_review/attest.py's hashing path between S3 and S4. That is a consistency (G4) concern about WHO threads the handle, not a G3 union-coverage gap — the corresponding parent obligation (AC1/AC2 + ref-coherence) is still CLAIMED by children (this child pins the SHA; S4 claims claim-gate ref-coherence). G3 union-coverage of the parent criteria is complete.

Conclusion: this child cleanly covers the materialization-core slice (faithful snapshot at pinned SHA, atomic/no-worktree concurrency safety, portability, fetch-credential errors) of the parent criteria; no parent acceptance/success criterion is left uncovered by the roster. No G3 finding for this pairing.

comments.body:
[G4 child-consistency — container review of parent 1f19-adf2-ffb2-4b09 paired with THIS child (S1 Faithful, lock-free snapshot materialization core)]

Ran the 7 cross-child interaction modes for this child against the WHOLE sibling roster (S2 70c7, S3 fa49, S4 30b3, S5 b7fa, S6 1ba5). One high-confidence finding (conflicting assumptions, mode 2) about WHERE the local-mode branch lives; the other 6 modes are clean under the high-confidence / benign-reading filter.

FINDING (conflicting assumptions — S1's materialize() claims a local-mode code path that S3 says never reaches materialize())
- finding: This child exposes `materialize(ref, *, source_mode) -> handle` with `source_mode` as a parameter, lists "descriptive fetch/auth errors" as part of the materialize() contract, and its AC5 reads "A private-repo fetch with missing credentials raises a descriptive, actionable error; **attested mode fails closed**" while the "What" bullet says "attested mode fails closed, **local mode still runs**". That phrasing places a per-mode branch (attested→fail-closed, local→still-runs) INSIDE materialize(), i.e. materialize() is entered in BOTH modes. S3 (fa49) contradicts this: its "What" says "Local: read the server's in-place checkout directly (**no materialization, no cache**)" and S2 (70c7) AC-narrative concurs ("local mode bypasses the cache: no materialization, no cache" per the S2 G3 note). If local never calls materialize(), then materialize()'s `source_mode` parameter and its "local mode still runs" error-branch are dead — and conversely, if materialize() does own the local branch, then S3's "no materialization" claim is false and the in-place-checkout read is implemented twice (once in materialize(), once in S3's gate dispatch).
- criteria: this child (6d56) AC5 + the `materialize(ref, *, source_mode)` signature + "Descriptive errors" What-bullet ("attested mode fails closed, local mode still runs"); S3 (fa49) "What" ("Local: read the server's in-place checkout directly (no materialization, no cache)") + AC2; parent AC3 ("local source mode reads the in-place checkout and CANNOT produce a signed verdict").
- location: 6d56 "What" (materialize signature + Descriptive-errors bullet) + AC5; fa49 "What" (Attested vs Local bullets) + Scope ("In: ... local-mode in-place read").
- evidence:
  1. 6d56 signature is `materialize(ref, *, source_mode) -> handle` — source_mode is a parameter of the materialize core, implying the core dispatches on it.
  2. 6d56 Descriptive-errors bullet: "attested mode fails closed, local mode still runs" — a local-mode behavior described as materialize()'s own responsibility.
  3. fa49 "What": "Local: read the server's in-place checkout directly (no materialization, no cache); mark the run as unsigned" — local mode explicitly does NOT enter materialization.
  4. fa49 Scope "In: ... local-mode in-place read" — the in-place read is claimed by S3, not S1.
  5. No sibling reconciles the two: neither ticket states whether materialize() is a no-op/short-circuit in local mode that returns a handle pointing at the in-place checkout, or whether S3 branches BEFORE calling materialize().
- scenarios: (a) Implementer of S1 builds source_mode dispatch + a local branch into materialize() that returns an in-place-checkout handle; implementer of S3 ALSO builds a local in-place read in the gate dispatch (its claimed Scope) → the in-place read is double-implemented, and the two may diverge on which root they read (snapshot tmp seam vs cfg.repo_path). (b) Implementer of S3 short-circuits local BEFORE materialize() per its "no materialization" wording; then S1's source_mode parameter and "local mode still runs" error-branch are dead code that no caller exercises, and S1 AC5's local clause cannot be tested through the real gate path.
- impact: Ambiguous ownership of the local-mode in-place-read path across the S1/S3 seam: parent AC3 ("local reads the in-place checkout") is claimed by S3, but S1's API surface and AC5 also reach into local mode, so the boundary "does materialize() run in local mode?" is answered oppositely by the two tickets. Either dead surface in S1 or double-implementation + possible read-root divergence in S3.
- checklist_item: conflicting assumptions (cross-child mode 2)
- suggested_fix: Pick one boundary and state it in both tickets. Preferred (matches S3's wording): S3 branches on source BEFORE calling S1 — local never enters materialize(); then drop `source_mode` from S1's `materialize()` signature (make it attested-only, e.g. `materialize(ref) -> handle`) and reword S1's Descriptive-errors bullet/AC5 to "attested mode fails closed" only, deleting the "local mode still runs" clause (that behavior belongs to S3). Alternatively, if materialize() is to own the local short-circuit, change S3 to "Local: call materialize(..., source_mode=local), which returns a handle over the in-place checkout (no fetch, no cache)" and remove S3's independent in-place-read claim, so the in-place read is implemented exactly once.

Clean on the other 6 modes:
- implicit shared state (cache on-disk layout / atomic-rename target): CLEAN. This child builds into `<root>/tmp/<uuid>/` and `rename()`s "into the cache path"; its AC3 leaves "only a tmp/ dir, cleaned on startup". S2 (70c7) owns that layout ("populated via S1's atomic dir-rename"; "Startup sweep: rmtree tmp/* trash/*"; locks/<sha>; gc/lock). Producer (atomic-rename primitive + tmp/ residue) → owner (cache root + startup sweep) is mutually named by both tickets (S1 Scope "Out: the cache/janitor (S2)"; S2 "populated via S1's atomic dir-rename"). Consistent contract, no divergence.
- dependency gap: CLEAN at this child. materialize()->handle is consumed by S2 (cache populate) and S3 (gate re-root), both naming S1. The standing roster dependency-gap (S4/S3 attest.py snapshot-handle routing) is a seam downstream of this child's resolved-SHA output, not originated here.
- scope overlap: CLEAN. Parent AC4 bundles atomic-population (this child AC3/AC6: GIT_INDEX_FILE temp index, no worktree, no index.lock/config.lock contention) + delete-on-last-close (S2 AC2). Split is clean; no double-ownership of the concurrency mechanism. (The local-read overlap is captured as the mode-2 finding above, not double-counted here.)
- ordering violation: CLEAN. All six children carry empty deps uniformly; parent AC8 only requires all-closed + green. The logical S1→S2/S3 ordering is not encoded, consistent with the roster-wide posture in prior reviews.
- consumer impact: CLEAN. This child's resolved-immutable-SHA + handle are consumed by S2 (cache key), S3 (re-root), S4 (sign over verified_at_sha), S5 (surface), S6 (document) — each claims its slice; the flock+mkdir-fallback portability seam S1 establishes is ridden by S2's cross-process flock(LOCK_EX)/gc-lock without re-owning it.
- residual references: CLEAN. "see S6" (credential message → S6 AC2), S2/S3/S4/S5 scope-outs (all present in roster), REBAR_GATE_TMPDIR (documented by S6 AC5), uploadpack.allowReachableSHA1InWant / --filter=blob:none (git config, real) all resolve.

Note: the materialization module (src/rebar/_engine/repo_snapshot.py) does not yet exist in the tree (pre-manifest epic; roster-wide search returns no repo_snapshot/allowReachableSHA1InWant), so the finding is grounded in the ticket-spec wording of the S1/S3 seam, not in shipped code.

Conclusion: one high-confidence cross-child inconsistency (conflicting assumptions, mode 2 — whether materialize() runs in local mode, answered oppositely by S1 and S3). The remaining 6 modes are clean.

S1's flock+atomic-mkdir-fallback portability seam vs S2's cross-process flock(LOCK_EX) on locks/<sha> + gc/lock: S1 ESTABLISHES the seam, S2's cross-process locks RIDE on it; no re-ownership of the tar/tmp/flock-fallback sub-clauses. LFS-pointer detection + defined submodule handling (S1 AC2) feed S4's "this is the code at SHA X" attestation faithfully; no downstream contract is broken by this child's surface.
- residual references: S6 (docs), S2 (cache/janitor), S3 (gate wiring), S4 (signing), REBAR_GATE_TMPDIR, uploadpack.allowReachableSHA1InWant, --filter=blob:none all resolve to real producers/trackers in the roster. No dangling reference.

Note (not a G4 finding on THIS pairing): the standing dependency-gap finding on sibling S4 (30b3) concerns WHO routes the pinned-SHA snapshot handle into src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py's dependency_hashes() / _rehash() / claim-gate freshness re-check — a code seam between S3 and S4. It introduces no inconsistency between S1 and the parent/siblings.

Conclusion: no high-confidence cross-child inconsistency between this materialization-core child and the parent/siblings. Empty G4 findings list for this pairing.

[G4 child-consistency — container review of parent 1f19-adf2-ffb2-4b09 paired with THIS child (S1 6d56: Faithful, lock-free snapshot materialization core)]

Ran the 7 cross-child interaction modes for this materialization-core child against the WHOLE sibling roster (S2 70c7 cache+janitor, S3 fa49 gate threading, S4 30b3 attested signing, S5 b7fa CLI/MCP surfacing, S6 1ba5 docs). ONE high-confidence finding (conflicting assumptions, mode 2, at the S1<->S3 local-mode seam); the other 6 modes are clean under the high-confidence / benign-reading filter.

FINDING (conflicting assumptions — does materialize() run in local mode? S1 says yes / S3 says local bypasses materialization entirely)
- finding: This child exposes `materialize(ref, *, source_mode) -> handle` — i.e. materialize() TAKES a `source_mode` (attested|local) argument and is the call site that owns the (only) lock-taking `git fetch origin` step. Its descriptive-errors design AND its AC5 are written for BOTH modes: "a fetch that fails on auth surfaces an actionable credential message ...; attested mode fails closed, LOCAL MODE STILL RUNS" (What, Descriptive errors bullet; AC5: "attested mode fails closed"). That phrasing only makes sense if materialize()/its fetch is INVOKED in local mode (and merely tolerates a failed fetch). But S3 (fa49), which owns the gate-side mode dispatch, states the OPPOSITE: "Local: read the server's in-place checkout directly (NO MATERIALIZATION, NO CACHE)" (What bullet 3) and "In local mode repo_root IS the read root (the checkout)" (AC4) — i.e. local mode never calls materialize() at all and never fetches. The two children encode contradictory assumptions about whether materialize() participates in local mode.
- criteria: this child (6d56) AC5 ("A private-repo fetch with missing credentials raises a descriptive, actionable error; attested mode fails closed") + its What ("materialize(ref, *, source_mode)"; "attested mode fails closed, local mode still runs"); S3 (fa49) AC4 ("local mode reads the in-place checkout ... repo_root IS the read root") + its What ("Local: ... no materialization, no cache"); parent AC3 (local source mode reads the in-place checkout) and AC6 (private-repo fetch failure surfaces a descriptive credential error).
- location: this child (6d56) What ("A core module ... exposing materialize(ref, *, source_mode) -> handle") + Descriptive-errors bullet ("attested mode fails closed, local mode still runs") + AC5; S3 (fa49) What bullet 3 ("Local: read the server's in-place checkout directly (no materialization, no cache); ... In local mode repo_root IS the read root") + AC4.
- evidence:
  1. This child's materialize() signature carries `source_mode` and its error behavior is specified per-mode ("attested mode fails closed, local mode still runs"), placing the local-mode branch (and the tolerated fetch) INSIDE the materialize() core that this child owns.
  2. S3 explicitly routes local mode AROUND materialize(): "Local: read the server's in-place checkout directly (no materialization, no cache)" and AC4 "In local mode repo_root IS the read root (the checkout); in attested mode repo_root only locates the object DB and the snapshot is the read root." Under S3, local never reaches materialize(), so there is no materialize()-side "local mode still runs" path to exercise.
  3. The two are not reconcilable as written: either local mode calls materialize(source_mode=local) (this child's model, where the fetch is attempted and tolerated on failure) OR local mode never calls materialize() (S3's model, in-place read only). The `source_mode=local` parameter on materialize() has no consumer if S3's "no materialization" path is authoritative; conversely S3's in-place-only local path leaves this child's "local mode still runs" error clause and the local branch of `source_mode` dead/contradicted.
- scenarios: (a) Implementer follows this child and makes materialize() the single entry for both modes (local => skip fetch / read in-place but still go through the handle) — then S3's AC4 "no materialization, no cache" and "repo_root IS the read root" are violated, and the cache/single-flight (S2) wrap a local dirty read it was never meant to touch. (b) Implementer follows S3 and bypasses materialize() for local — then this child's `materialize(ref, *, source_mode)` local branch and its "local mode still runs" error clause are unreachable, and the descriptive-error fail-open-in-local behavior this child's AC5 implies has no home (S3's in-place path raises no fetch error at all). Either way one child's stated contract is contradicted.
- impact: The local-mode ownership boundary between this child (materialize core, which advertises a `source_mode` param and a local-still-runs error path) and S3 (gate dispatch, which says local does no materialization) is contradictory. Parent AC3 (local reads in-place) and AC6/this-child-AC5 (descriptive fetch error, fail-closed-attested-vs-local) are decomposed onto two children that disagree on WHETHER materialize() is in the local path, so the local-mode behavior is under-determined.
- checklist_item: conflicting assumptions (cross-child mode 2) at the S1<->S3 local-mode seam
- suggested_fix: Reconcile the local-mode contract in ONE place. Preferred: drop the `source_mode` arg from this child's materialize() (make materialize() attested-only — it always fetches+pins+materializes) and let S3 own the mode branch (attested => call materialize(); local => in-place read, never call materialize()), moving this child's "local mode still runs" error clause to S3's local path; restate this child's AC5 as purely the attested fail-closed credential error. Alternatively, if materialize() is to remain the single entry for both modes, update S3's What/AC4 to call materialize(source_mode=local) (and define how "no cache / repo_root IS read root" is honored inside materialize) so the two children share one model. Either way, exactly one of the two children should own the local-vs-attested branch and the other's wording must match.

Clean on the other 6 modes:
- implicit shared state: the resolved immutable SHA (this child resolves ref->SHA against origin), the snapshot dir handle, and the portability seam (REBAR_GATE_TMPDIR, flock+mkdir fallback) all have this child as the single producer; consumers are S2 (caches the entry via this child's atomic dir-rename), S3 (re-roots the gate onto the handle), S4 (signs over the SHA), S5 (surfaces it), S6 (documents the knobs). Consistent producer->consumer chain; no shared value without a producer. (The attest.py-hashing-basis routing is a separate standing S3<->S4 seam, not this child's.)
- dependency gap: this child's Scope cleanly delegates downstream (cache/janitor S2, gate wiring S3, signing S4, surfaces S5) and the credential-MESSAGE docs to S6 (its "see S6" ref resolves to S6 AC2); each named consumer exists in the roster. No unowned downstream.
- scope overlap: faithful materialization / atomic population (this child) vs caching+single-flight+eviction (S2) is a clean producer/consumer split — S2 explicitly scopes "materialization itself (S1)" out and populates "via S1's atomic dir-rename". No double-implementation.
- ordering violation: the encoded dep graph (S1->{S2,S3}, etc.) points the benign way — this child is the upstream producer the others build on; no contradiction, and parent AC8 only requires all-closed + green.
- consumer impact: this child's handle/SHA/portability-seam contracts are consumed by S2/S3/S4/S5/S6 without redefinition; the fetch-prerequisite notes (uploadpack.allowReachableSHA1InWant, --filter=blob:none) are this child's own implementation guidance, not a contract a sibling must satisfy.
- residual references: S2/S3/S4/S5/S6 scope-out refs, REBAR_GATE_TMPDIR (documented by S6), the LFS/submodule handling, and the in-toto/SHA hand-offs all resolve to real roster siblings/trackers. No dangling reference.

Note (not a G4 finding on THIS pairing): the standing dependency-gap on sibling S4 (30b3) — routing the pinned-SHA snapshot handle into plan_review/attest.py's dependency_hashes()/_rehash()/freshness re-check — is a code seam between S3 and S4; it consumes this child's handle but the unowned routing lives in the S3<->S4 seam, not in this materialization-core child.

[PASS 1 review — T3 Integration feasibility — S1 materialize core (the integration-bearing child)]

AFFIRMATIONS (T3 criteria this slice satisfies):
- technical_feasibility / named-subcommand existence — MATCH. The git plumbing this child depends on (`git fetch origin`, `git read-tree <sha>` into a temp index via `GIT_INDEX_FILE`, `git checkout-index --all --prefix=<tmp>/`, ref→immutable-SHA resolution) are all real git subcommands, and git-as-subprocess is an ALREADY-USED integration in this repo (e.g. src/rebar/llm/code_review.py:53 `_git()`, src/rebar/signing.py:300 `head_sha()` via `git rev-parse`, src/rebar/_store/sync.py `_git`). Extending that established subprocess seam to read-tree/checkout-index is feasible as described — not a new external capability class.
- auth/HTTPS + fetch preconditions stated — PASS. The plan pre-empts the arbitrary-SHA fetch precondition explicitly ("arbitrary-SHA fetch needs `uploadpack.allowReachableSHA1InWant` (or fetch a containing ref then resolve); prefer `--filter=blob:none`"), and AC5 routes the private-repo credential-failure path to a descriptive error with attested fail-closed. This is exactly the auth-precondition statement T3 asks for.
- portability primitives — PASS. `fcntl.flock` + atomic-`mkdir` fallback and `tempfile.gettempdir()`/`mkdtemp` are stdlib; no external `tar` dependency. AC4 nails this down.

FINDINGS:

- [ ] LFS-pointer detection and submodule materialization are asserted as in-scope capabilities (AC2) but the git mechanism is left UNVERIFIED — name the concrete command(s) or route a SPIKE. (criteria: T3 technical_feasibility; T3 named-subcommand existence = UNVERIFIED) — location: 6d56 "What" (FAITHFUL tree materialization bullet) + AC2 ("LFS pointer files are detected (not silently served as content); submodule handling is defined and documented"). evidence: The chosen `git read-tree <sha>` + `git checkout-index --all` path materializes the committed tree's blob CONTENT — which for an LFS-tracked path IS the pointer file, and for a submodule is a gitlink (mode 160000) that checkout-index does not populate with the submodule's working content. So AC2's "LFS pointer files are detected (not silently served as content)" and "submodule handling is defined" are precisely the cases the read-tree/checkout-index primitive does NOT resolve on its own; the plan asserts the outcome (detected/handled) without naming HOW (e.g. parse `.gitattributes` filter=lfs + the 130-byte `version https://git-lfs…` pointer header; enumerate gitlinks via `git ls-tree` and decide fetch-vs-skip). This is an asserted-but-unverified capability the faithfulness guarantee (AC1) leans on. suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the "What" naming the LFS-detection mechanism (e.g. detect via `.gitattributes` `filter=lfs` and/or the LFS pointer-file magic header; document whether real LFS content is fetched or the pointer is surfaced as a known limitation) and the submodule mechanism (e.g. `git ls-tree` gitlink enumeration; document recurse-vs-omit), or split LFS/submodule faithfulness into a short SPIKE child since it is the one materialization sub-capability whose feasibility is not yet pinned to a named command.

NOTE (not a finding, for the parent epic's "What"/Context): the GitHub tarball API is cited in the parent Context as a design influence and is explicitly placed Out of scope ("Sparse archiving"; the materialize path is git-plumbing-only, no HTTPS tarball fetch). Since no child actually calls that API, there is no asserted-but-unverified external endpoint here — T3 not-applicable to the tarball reference. Recorded so a later pass doesn't re-raise it as a missing-auth/endpoint gap.

comments.body:
## Completion verification — PASS

All 6 acceptance criteria are demonstrably met by the implementation in `src/rebar/_snapshot/` (package) and `tests/unit/test_repo_snapshot.py`.

### Per-criterion findings

**AC1 — `materialize(ref)` produces a directory byte-matching the committed tree INCLUDING `export-ignore`-marked files (faithful, not `git archive`)**
MET. `_materialize_tree()` (lines 433–467 of `repo_snapshot.py`) runs `git read-tree <sha>` into a throwaway `GIT_INDEX_FILE` then `git checkout-index --all --prefix=<dest>/`. The module docstring at lines 14–21 explicitly states: "export-ignore files ARE present and export-subst is NOT applied". Test `test_materialize_byte_matches_committed_tree_including_export_ignore` (line 58 of the test file) commits a file with `export-ignore` in `.gitattributes` and an `export-subst` file, then asserts both survive byte-for-byte in the snapshot.

**AC2 — LFS pointer files are detected (not silently served as content); submodule handling is defined and documented**
MET. LFS detection uses a magic-header check (`_LFS_POINTER_MAGIC = b"version https://git-lfs.github.com/spec/v1"`, line 68), implemented in `is_lfs_pointer()` (lines 381–388) and `_detect_lfs_pointers()` (lines 407–415). Submodules are enumerated via `git ls-tree` mode-160000 filtering in `_list_submodules()` (lines 391–404). Both caveats are persisted on a sidecar file (lines 158–173) so cross-process cache hits stay correct. The module docstring (lines 23–29) documents the behaviour explicitly ("never silently wrong"). Tests at lines 97–134 exercise both paths.

**AC3 — Population is atomic; a crashed materialization leaves only a `tmp/` dir, cleaned on startup**
MET. `materialize()` builds into `<root>/tmp/build-<sha>-<uuid>/`, fsyncs (lines 550–561), then `os.rename()` into the content-addressed `<root>/<sha>` destination; on any exception the partial build is removed (lines 570–578). `sweep_tmp()` (lines 470–491) removes all entries under `<root>/tmp/`. Tests `test_no_tmp_left_after_successful_materialize` (line 141) and `test_sweep_tmp_clears_crashed_build` (line 151) verify both halves.

**AC4 — No external `tar` binary; no hardcoded `/tmp`; runs on macOS + Linux**
MET. The word `tar` does not appear anywhere in the implementation. `store_root()` (lines 125–138) reads `REBAR_GATE_TMPDIR` from the environment, falling back to `tempfile.gettempdir()` — no hardcoded `/tmp`. `fcntl.flock` is imported with an `ImportError` fallback to an atomic `os.mkdir` spin-lock (lines 61–64, 214–250), covering both POSIX platforms. Test `test_store_root_honors_gate_tmpdir` (line 174) verifies the `REBAR_GATE_TMPDIR` override.

**AC5 — A private-repo fetch with missing credentials raises a descriptive, actionable error; attested mode fails closed**
MET. `_fetch_origin()` sets `GIT_TERMINAL_PROMPT=0` (line 318) so missing credentials fail immediately rather than hanging. `_is_auth_failure()` (lines 303–305) matches against `_AUTH_STDERR_MARKERS` (lines 83–95) and raises `SnapshotFetchError` with a full credential-remedy message ("Configure a git credential helper, a deploy key, or a token…", lines 337–343). Any other fetch failure also raises `SnapshotFetchError` (lines 344–347), ensuring attested mode is always fail-closed. Tests at lines 185–208 (including the security regression test `test_malicious_ref_in_targeted_fetch_does_not_execute` at line 211 verifying the `--end-of-options` RCE fix).

**AC6 — Concurrent `materialize()` of different SHAs do not contend on `index.lock`/`config.lock`**
MET. `_materialize_tree()` creates a per-build throwaway `GIT_INDEX_FILE` (line 441), so `git read-tree` and `git checkout-index` never touch the repo's own index lock. In-process coalescing serialises only the fetch step (`_fetch_lock_for()`, lines 259–267); materializations of different SHAs run fully in parallel. Test `test_concurrent_different_shas_no_index_contention` (line 239) uses `ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=2)` and asserts `git status --porcelain` is unchanged after concurrent materializations — the load-bearing assertion at line 260 ("Drop GIT_INDEX_FILE and this regresses").

### Deviation from ticket spec (no AC impact)
The ticket suggested `src/rebar/_engine/repo_snapshot.py`; the implementation chose `src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py` instead. The implementation comment explains why (`_engine/` is package data, not an importable Python package). All behavioral ACs are satisfied regardless of the path chosen.

# Faithful, lock-free snapshot materialization core

## Why

The gates need a real on-disk tree at a pinned SHA, but the server's worktree is mutable and
shared. `git archive` is lossy (drops `export-ignore` files, omits submodules, returns LFS
pointer files, rewrites `export-subst`) — unacceptable for an attestation that claims "this
is the code at SHA X". We need a faithful, concurrency-safe, portable materialization.

## What

A core module (e.g. `src/rebar/_engine/repo_snapshot.py`) exposing `materialize(ref, *,
source_mode) -> handle` (context-managed):
- Coalesced `git fetch origin` (the only lock-taking step); resolve `ref` (branch|tag|SHA)
  to an immutable SHA against origin.
- FAITHFUL tree materialization: `git read-tree <sha>` into a temp index (`GIT_INDEX_FILE`)
  then `git checkout-index --all --prefix=<tmp>/` — exact committed tree, no worktree, no
  `export-ignore` drops.
- Atomic population: build into `<root>/tmp/<uuid>/`, fsync files, `rename()` the directory
  into the cache path, fsync parent dir; readers see all-or-nothing.
- Portability: extraction never shells to external `tar`; temp root from
  `tempfile.gettempdir()`/`mkdtemp` (0700), overridable via `REBAR_GATE_TMPDIR`, created
  OUTSIDE the repo/.git; `fcntl.flock` with atomic-`mkdir` fallback on a local FS.
- Descriptive errors: a fetch that fails on auth surfaces an actionable credential message
  (see S6), not a raw git error; attested mode fails closed, local mode still runs.
- Fetch prerequisites documented: arbitrary-SHA fetch needs `uploadpack.allowReachableSHA1InWant`
  (or fetch a containing ref then resolve); prefer `--filter=blob:none` over deep shallow refetch.

## Scope

- In: the materialize() core, faithful tree extraction, atomic population, portability seam,
  fetch-coalescing, descriptive fetch/auth errors.
- Out: the cache/janitor (S2), gate wiring (S3), signing (S4), surfaces (S5).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `materialize(ref)` produces a directory whose contents byte-match the committed tree at
      the resolved SHA, INCLUDING `export-ignore`-marked files (faithful, not `git archive`).
- [ ] LFS pointer files are detected (not silently served as content); submodule handling is
      defined and documented.
- [ ] Population is atomic: a reader never observes a partial tree; a crashed materialization
      leaves only a `tmp/` dir, cleaned on startup.
- [ ] No external `tar` binary is invoked; no hardcoded `/tmp`; runs on macOS + Linux.
- [ ] A private-repo fetch with missing credentials raises a descriptive, actionable error;
      attested mode fails closed.
- [ ] Concurrent `materialize()` of different SHAs do not contend on `index.lock`/`config.lock`.
